claims.flip_evidence_text: provided further that, with respect to clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv)(A), (vi) and (vii)(B), such events, changes, developments, circumstances, facts or effects (as the case may be) that are not otherwise excluded from the definition hereof may be taken into account in determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be expected to occur to the extent (and, for the avoidance of doubt, only to the extent) that they disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries (taken as a whole) relative to other similarly situated and comparable companies in the industries and in the geographic markets in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct their businesses.
claims.notes: The claim correctly states the general exclusion under clause (i) but is incomplete because it omits the disproportionality carve-back, which allows such changes to be considered if they disproportionately adversely affect the Company relative to similarly situated and comparable companies.

prompt:
You are a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list.

For each claim return:
- correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
- load_bearing_label: yes or no
- flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
- notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt:
“Material Adverse Effect” means any event, change, development, circumstance, fact or effect that, individually or in the aggregate, is materially adverse to the financial condition, business or operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries (taken as a whole); provided, however, that no event, change, development, circumstance, fact or effect resulting from any of the following shall constitute or be taken into account in determining whether a Material Adverse Effect has occurred or would reasonably be expected to occur: (i) any changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions; (ii) changes in the credit, capital, securities or financial markets, commodity prices, inflation or United States or global regulatory or business conditions; (iii) changes or developments in the industries in which the Company or any of its Subsidiaries operate or the industries to which the Company and its Subsidiaries sell their products or services; (iv) (A) changes, proposed changes, pending changes or changes in interpretation or enforcement of GAAP or in any Law (including any Healthcare Law) or rules or regulations promulgated by any Governmental Entity (including any contractor engaged by a Governmental Entity) or (B) COVID-19 Measures; (v) any failure by the Company to meet any internal, public or other projections or forecasts or estimates of revenues or earnings for any period; provided that the underlying cause of such failure may (to the extent not otherwise excluded under this definition) be taken into account in determining whether there is, has been or would reasonably be expected to be a Material Adverse Effect; (vi) acts of war (whether or not declared), any outbreak or escalation of hostilities, geopolitical conditions, tariffs, sanctions, trade wars political unrest, civil disobedience, protests, public demonstrations, acts of armed hostility, sabotage, terrorism, cyberterrorism, cyberattack (to the extent not specifically targeting the Company), military, paramilitary or police actions, or national or international calamity, or the escalation or worsening of any of the foregoing or any response by any Governmental Entity to any of the foregoing; (vii) (A) any epidemic, pandemic, outbreak of illness or other public health event (including COVID-19) or the escalation or worsening of any of the foregoing or quarantine restriction or (B) any weather event, flood, volcanic eruption, earthquake, nuclear incident or other natural or man-made disaster or other force majeure event or occurrence or the escalation or worsening of any of the foregoing; (viii) the taking of any action required by this Agreement (except for the Company’s obligations set forth in Section 7.1(a)) or the failure to take any action prohibited by, this Agreement; (ix) any decline in the market price or trading volume of the Shares on the NYSE; provided that the underlying cause of such decline (to the extent not otherwise excluded under this definition) may be taken into account in determining whether there is, has been or would reasonably be expected to be a Material Adverse Effect; (x) changes caused by the negotiation, execution, announcement, or (except for the Company’s obligations set forth in Section 7.1(a)) performance of this Agreement or the pendency or consummation of the transactions contemplated by this Agreement, including any loss or change in relationship with any employee, officer, director, customer, supplier, vendor, reseller, distributor, or other business partner of the Company or any of its Subsidiaries (it being understood that this clause shall not apply with respect to any representation or warranty set forth in Section 5.4(a); (xi) the commencement or pendency of any litigation alleging breach of fiduciary duty or similar claim or violation of law relating to this Agreement or the transactions contemplated thereby; or (xii) the identity of, or any other facts specific to, Parent or any of its Affiliates as the acquiror of the Company; provided further that, with respect to clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv)(A), (vi) and (vii)(B), such events, changes, developments, circumstances, facts or effects (as the case may be) that are not otherwise excluded from the definition hereof may be taken into account in determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be expected to occur to the extent (and, for the avoidance of doubt, only to the extent) that they disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries (taken as a whole) relative to other similarly situated and comparable companies in the industries and in the geographic markets in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct their businesses. (Pages 16-17)

Question:
General economic and financial conditions (Y/N)

Llama answer:
No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect.

Canonical claims:
- claim-c7a23c7ecc36: No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect.

(Pages 16-17)

No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect.

message.reasoning:
The user wants me to act as a proxy legal annotator for a research pilot. I need to evaluate the canonical claim against the contract excerpt, question, and model answer.

Let me break down what I need to do:
1. For each claim (there's only one here: claim-c7a23c7ecc36), return:
   - correctness_label: true, false, or partially_true
   - load_bearing_label: yes or no
   - flip_evidence_text: required if load_bearing_label=yes, else empty string
   - notes: optional short note

Contract excerpt analysis:
The definition of "Material Adverse Effect" excludes:
- (i) any changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions
- (ii) changes in the credit, capital, securities or financial markets, commodity prices, inflation or United States or global regulatory or business conditions
- There's also a carve-back for clauses (i), (ii), (iii), etc. that allows consideration to the extent they disproportionately adversely affect the Company relative to similarly situated companies.

Question: "General economic and financial conditions (Y/N)"
This seems to be asking whether general economic and financial conditions constitute a Material Adverse Effect.

Llama answer: "No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect."

Canonical claim: "No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect."

Evaluation:
- correctness_label: The claim is mostly true but incomplete because it ignores the carve-back in the "provided further" clause. The exclusion is not absolute - if the changes disproportionately adversely affect the Company relative to similarly situated companies, they CAN be taken into account. So saying they are "excluded" without qualification is not fully accurate. However, looking at the claim, it says "changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded" - which is what clause (i) says, but subject to the carve-back. Is the claim partially_true because it misses the disproportionality carve-back? 

Wait, the Llama answer and canonical claim say they are excluded, which is generally true as a baseline, but the full answer should note the exception. In legal annotation tasks like this, usually if the answer states something as an absolute exclusion but the contract has a carve-back, the answer is considered partially_true or false depending on the context.

Let me think carefully. The claim says: "No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded from constituting a Material Adverse Effect."

This is a direct reference to clause (i). However, the "provided further" clause says that with respect to clauses (i), (ii), etc., such events may be taken into account to the extent they disproportionately adversely affect the Company relative to similarly situated companies.

So the claim is not fully correct because it states an absolute exclusion when there is a disproportionality carve-back. However, in many legal QA contexts, if the question is simply whether general economic conditions are excluded, the primary answer is "Yes, excluded" and the carve-back is a nuance. But the instructions say to label true, false, or partially_true.

Given that the carve-back is an important qualifier, the claim is at least partially_true. But is it false? The claim says they are "excluded" - which is the general rule, but not the complete rule. "Partially_true" seems most appropriate because the exclusion exists but is subject to the disproportionality exception.

However, looking at the Llama answer and canonical claim, they mirror each other exactly. The question asks "General economic and financial conditions (Y/N)". The answer addresses "economic or political conditions" from clause (i), but "financial conditions" could also relate to clause (ii) (credit, capital, securities or financial markets). But the claim focuses on clause (i).

Wait, the question is "General economic and financial conditions (Y/N)". This is ambiguous - it might be asking whether these are excluded. The answer says "No" meaning they do not constitute MAE. But actually, the question might just be a topic label, and the answer should be "No" as in "No, they are not a MAE" or "No" as in answering the Y/N question. The Llama answer says "No" and then explains.

Let me reconsider correctness. The claim says changes in general US or global economic or political conditions are excluded. This is true as far as clause (i) goes. The carve-back allows them to be taken into account only if disproportionate. So they are generally excluded. Is the claim false for missing the carve-back? In strict legal annotation, usually yes, it's partially_true or false. But I need to decide.

Actually, looking at standard practices in this domain, if a claim asserts an absolute legal proposition but there is a material exception, the claim is typically labeled partially_true if the core of the claim is correct but incomplete. If the exception completely undermines the claim, it might be false. Here, the exclusion is the general rule, and the carve-back is a narrow exception ("only to the extent disproportionate"). So the claim captures the general rule but omits the exception. I think partially_true is correct.

Load bearing: Does the claim contain load-bearing content that, if flipped, would change the answer? The claim states that these conditions are excluded. If this were false (i.e., if they were NOT excluded), the answer would fundamentally change. So yes, the claim is load-bearing.

Flip evidence text: If load_bearing=yes, I need to provide the text that would flip the claim. The flip would be the carve-back text or the clause showing it's not an absolute exclusion. The relevant text would be the "provided further" clause: "provided further that, with respect to clauses (i) ... such events ... may be taken into account ... to the extent ... they disproportionately adversely affect the Company..."

Wait, the flip evidence text should be the text that supports the opposite of the claim. The claim says they are excluded. To flip this, we need text showing they are NOT absolutely excluded - i.e., the carve-back. So the flip evidence text should be the proviso at the end.

Let me draft the flip evidence_text: "provided further that, with respect to clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv)(A), (vi) and (vii)(B), such events, changes, developments, circumstances, facts or effects (as the case may be) that are not otherwise excluded from the definition hereof may be taken into account in determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be expected to occur to the extent (and, for the avoidance of doubt, only to the extent) that they disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries (taken as a whole) relative to other similarly situated and comparable companies in the industries and in the geographic markets in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct their businesses."

Actually, looking more carefully at the prompt, I need to provide flip_evidence_text only if load_bearing_label=yes. The flip evidence text should presumably be the text from the contract that contradicts or flips the claim.

Wait, is the claim actually true? Let me re-read the question. "General economic and financial conditions (Y/N)". The Llama answer says "No". The claim says "No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded..."

Is the claim fully true? The question asks about "General economic and financial conditions". The claim only mentions "economic or political conditions" from clause (i), but "financial conditions" could map to clause (ii). However, the claim is still directionally correct. But the omission of the carve-back is significant.

I think I should go with:
- correctness_label: partially_true (because it misses the disproportionality carve-back)
- load_bearing_label: yes (the exclusion is the core of the answer)
- flip_evidence_text: the carve-back proviso
- notes: mention that the exclusion is subject to the disproportionality carve-back in the "provided further" clause

But wait, looking at similar tasks, sometimes if the claim is substantively correct but incomplete regarding an exception, it might be labeled "true" if the exception is viewed as secondary, or "partially_true" if it's material. Given that the carve-back is a full sentence proviso and is important in MAE clauses, I think partially_true is safer.

However, let me reconsider the question format. The question is extremely terse: "General economic and financial conditions (Y/N)". The answer is "No...". The claim is essentially the answer. In many of these datasets, the goal is to see if the claim accurately reflects the model answer and the contract. The model answer says what it says. But we are to evaluate correctness against the contract.

Actually, the user says: "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." So I should evaluate whether the claim is correct based on the contract.

Let me check if there's any ambiguity about "load_bearing". In this context, "load-bearing" usually means that the claim contains the central piece of information that answers the question. If you remove or flip it, the answer changes. Here, the central piece is that general economic/political conditions are excluded. That's load-bearing.

The flip evidence text should be the text that, if cited, would flip the load-bearing part. So if the claim says they are excluded, the flip evidence is the text showing they might not be excluded (the carve-back).

Let me format the output. It seems the user wants a structured output for each claim.

Wait, the instructions say "For each claim return: ...". I should probably return it in a clear format, perhaps JSON or a structured list.

Let me check the exact claim ID: claim-c7a23c7ecc36

I will provide:
correctness_label: partially_true
load_bearing_label: yes
flip_evidence_text: "provided further that, with respect to clauses (i), (ii), (iii), (iv)(A), (vi) and (vii)(B), such events, changes, developments, circumstances, facts or effects (as the case may be) that are not otherwise excluded from the definition hereof may be taken into account in determining whether a “Material Adverse Effect” has occurred or would reasonably be expected to occur to the extent (and, for the avoidance of doubt, only to the extent) that they disproportionately adversely affect the Company and its Subsidiaries (taken as a whole) relative to other similarly situated and comparable companies in the industries and in the geographic markets in which the Company and its Subsidiaries conduct their businesses."
notes: The claim correctly identifies the general exclusion under clause (i) but omits the disproportionality carve-back, which permits such changes to be considered if they disproportionately affect the Company relative to similarly situated companies.

Actually, looking at it again, maybe the claim is considered true because the exclusion is the baseline rule, and the carve-back is an exception. But in strict legal reading, stating an exclusion as absolute when it's qualified is inaccurate. I'll stick with partially_true.

One more thought: The question asks "General economic and financial conditions (Y/N)". The claim says "No, changes in general United States or global economic or political conditions are excluded". It doesn't mention "financial conditions" from clause (ii). The question covers both economic and financial. The claim only mentions clause (i) (economic/political) but not clause (ii) (financial markets, etc.). However, the Llama answer also only mentions clause (i). The claim tracks the Llama answer. But the question might be interpreted as covering both. Still, the claim is about the topic. I think partially_true is still appropriate due to the carve-back omission.

Let me reconsider if it should be "false". If I say "X is excluded" but the contract says "X is excluded except when Y", then saying "X is excluded" without qualification is false as a blanket statement. In law, exceptions matter a lot. However, in natural language, people often state the general rule. The annotation guidelines usually prefer partially_true for incomplete but directionally correct statements.

I'll go with partially_true.

Wait, looking at the prompt again: "Use only the contract excerpt, the question, the model answer, and the fixed claim list." This suggests I should also consider the model answer. The model answer is the Llama answer. The canonical claim is identical to the Llama answer. But I need to evaluate correctness of the claim. Does the model answer being identical mean the claim is "true" to the model answer? The task is likely evaluating claims against the contract, not just the model answer. The phrase "proxy legal annotator" suggests I'm checking if the claim is legally correct based on the contract.

I think my analysis holds. Let me format the output cleanly.

Actually, I need to make sure about the flip_evidence_text. Should it be the exact text from the contract? Yes, typically. I should copy it exactly.

Also, I should check if the claim might be considered "true" because the question is simply asking if general economic/financial conditions are excluded, and the answer is generally yes, they are excluded. But the presence of the carve-back makes it qualified.

I'll provide the output in a structured format.
